message.content: ["Two simple driving mechanisms can universally simulate any local active dynamics in spin systems: a many-body Brownian pump and a many-body Brownian ratchet.", "Using probabilistic cellular automata, it is proven that for any local active dynamics, there is always a many-body Brownian ratchet or pump that approximates the dynamics up to noise that can be made arbitrarily weak.", "A ferromagnetic Ising ratchet on a bilayer lattice serves as a robust classical memory even under a symmetry-breaking field.", "Ratchets can use steady heat currents to autonomously generate and stabilize novel collective behavior, realizing a new static setting for nonequilibrium many-body dynamics."]
message.reasoning_content:
We are asked to split the text into facets: separate aspects, conditions, or sub-claims. Output a JSON array of 2-4 short strings. Each string should be a self-contained facet in the text's own terms. No invention, no overlap. If only one facet, return one element.

Let's parse the text:

- The text talks about Brownian ratchets and pumps simulating many-body active dynamics. 
- It mentions that active systems exhibit phenomena forbidden in equilibrium.
- It states that two simple driving mechanisms can universally simulate any local active dynamics in spin systems: a many-body Brownian pump (time-periodic Hamiltonian with cold bath) and a many-body Brownian ratchet (static Hamiltonian with hot and cold baths, steady heat current drives transport and local active dynamics).
- It proves using probabilistic cellular automata that for any continuous-time or discrete-time local active dynamics, there is a many-body Brownian ratchet or pump that approximates it, up to arbitrarily weak noise.
- It demonstrates a concrete example: a ferromagnetic Ising ratchet on a bilayer lattice that serves as a robust classical memory even under a symmetry-breaking field.
- Broader implication: ratchets can use steady heat currents to generate and stabilize novel collective behavior, a new static setting for nonequilibrium many-body dynamics.

We need to break this into distinct facets. The facets should be self-contained, i.e., each is a complete statement. They should be in the text's own terms. We should avoid overlap.

Possible facets:
1. The claim that two simple driving mechanisms can universally simulate any local active dynamics in spin systems. (This is a main claim.)
2. The definition/description of the two mechanisms: many-body Brownian pump and many-body Brownian ratchet. (But this might be part of the claim.)
3. The proof using probabilistic cellular automata: for any local active dynamics, there exists a ratchet or pump that approximates it with arbitrarily weak noise. (Another facet.)
4. The concrete demonstration: ferromagnetic Ising ratchet as robust classical memory. (A demonstration facet.)
5. The broader implication: ratchets can autonomously generate and stabilize novel collective behavior, a new static setting. (A broader conclusion.) 

But we need 2-4. So we can combine some. The text's structure: universal simulation claim, proof, example, broader impact. Each is distinct. Maybe facets: (1) Universal simulation by two mechanisms, (2) Proof of approximation up to weak noise, (3) Concrete Ising ratchet example as robust memory, (4) Broader implications. But check for overlap. The example is a demonstration of the claim, so it's a sub-claim. The proof supports the claim. So they are distinct. 

We must extract "facets" as aspects or conditions or sub-claims. So I'll list them:

- "Two simple driving mechanisms can universally simulate any local active dynamics in spin systems: a many-body Brownian pump and a many-body Brownian ratchet."
- "Using probabilistic cellular automata, it is proven that for any local active dynamics, there is always a many-body Brownian ratchet or pump that approximates the dynamics up to noise that can be made arbitrarily weak."
- "A ferromagnetic Ising ratchet on a bilayer lattice serves as a robust classical memory even under a symmetry-breaking field."
- "Ratchets can use steady heat currents to autonomously generate and stabilize novel collective behavior, realizing a new static setting for nonequilibrium many-body dynamics."

These are 4. No overlapping. They are self-contained. Use the text's own phrasing as much as possible.

JSON array: ["Two simple driving mechanisms can universally simulate any local active dynamics in spin systems: a many-body Brownian pump and a many-body Brownian ratchet.", "Using probabilistic cellular automata, it is proven that for any local active dynamics, there is always a many-body Brownian ratchet or pump that approximates the dynamics up to noise that can be made arbitrarily weak.", "A ferromagnetic Ising ratchet on a bilayer lattice serves as a robust classical memory even under a symmetry-breaking field.", "Ratchets can use steady heat currents to autonomously generate and stabilize novel collective behavior, realizing a new static setting for nonequilibrium many-body dynamics."]

Check: Text mentions "Here we show that two simple driving mechanisms can universally simulate any local active dynamics in spin systems." So first facet captures that. Second: "Using probabilistic cellular automata as an explicit model, we prove that for any continuous-time (or discrete-time) local active dynamics, there is always a many-body Brownian ratchet (or pump) that approximates the dynamics, up to noise that can be made arbitrarily weak by tuning energy scales and other parameters." So second facet is the proof. Third: "As a concrete demonstration, we construct a simple ferromagnetic Ising ratchet on a bilayer lattice. When the two layers are coupled to baths at different temperatures, this model serves as a robust classical memory even under a symmetry-breaking field..." Fourth: "More broadly, our work shows that ratchets can use steady heat currents to autonomously generate and stabilize novel collective behavior, realizing a new static setting for nonequilibrium many-body dynamics."
